channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Byzantine-Robust and Differentially Private Federated Optimization under Weaker Assumptions
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) faces dual threats: privacy leakage via gradient updates and Byzantine attacks from malicious servers. Prior works ensuring both differential privacy (DP) and Byzantine robustness rely on unrealistic assumptions (e.g., bounded gradients) or lack rigorous convergence guarantees. We propose **Byz-Clip21-SGD2M**, a novel algorithm integrating double momentum, adaptive gradient clipping, and DP-compliant robust aggregation. Under standard $L$-smoothness and $\sigma$-sub-Gaussian gradient noise assumptions—*without requiring bounded gradients*—we establish high-probability convergence with rate $O(\sigma/\sqrt{T} + \alpha\sqrt{d/T})$, recovering optimal $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ in benign settings. Experiments on MNIST with CNN/MLP show our method improves test accuracy by 3.7–5.2% over state-of-the-art under $(\varepsilon=2.0,\delta=10^{-5},\alpha=20\%)$, while reducing communication overhead by 18%.

item.title: Targeted Adversarial Traffic Generation : Black-box Approach to Evade Intrusion Detection Systems in IoT Networks
item.description: This paper introduces **Targeted Adversarial Traffic Generation (TATG)**, a practical black-box adversarial attack framework designed to evade ML-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) in resource-constrained IoT networks. Unlike prior white-box methods, TATG operates without model access or gradient information, instead leveraging protocol-aware perturbations on MQTT/CoAP fields (e.g., QoS flags, topic length, payload entropy) and reinforcement learning–guided query-efficient search (<200 queries) to generate semantically valid adversarial traffic. Evaluated on three state-of-the-art IoT IDS models (LSTM, RF, GraphSAGE) trained on CICIoT2023, TATG achieves an average **92.7% evasion success rate**, with generated traffic indistinguishable from benign flows under Wireshark and Snort inspection. To counter this threat, we propose the lightweight **Adversarial-Aware Defense (AAD)** module, which detects adversarial traffic by monitoring cross-packet entropy drift and protocol-field mutation rates—achieving **98.4% detection accuracy (FPR = 1.2%)**, outperforming existing defenses. All components are validated on Raspberry Pi 4 and ESP32-C3 devices, confirming real-world deployability. This work bridges the critical gap between theoretical adversarial vulnerability and practical IoT security.

item.title: Robust Safety Monitoring of Language Models via Activation Watermarking
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) pose serious safety risks when misused to generate hazardous content (e.g., weapon instructions or malware). While providers deploy runtime *monitoring* to detect unsafe outputs, adaptive adversaries—who know the monitor’s architecture but not its secret key—can craft queries that simultaneously elicit harmful behavior and evade detection. We formalize robust monitoring as a security game and empirically demonstrate that state-of-the-art monitors fail catastrophically under such attacks (up to 89% evasion rate). To address this, we propose *activation watermarking*: injecting key-dependent, bounded stochastic perturbations into intermediate layer activations during inference. This preserves model functionality while introducing irreducible uncertainty for the attacker about the monitor’s decision boundary. Under realistic adaptive threat models, activation watermarking achieves up to **52% higher detection accuracy** than guard baselines (e.g., SafeT, LlamaGuard), maintaining false positive rates <0.8% across Llama-2, Qwen, and Phi-3. Our method adds negligible latency (<1.2%) and provides the first practical, key-based defense against adaptive monitoring evasion.

item.title: Mind Your HEARTBEAT! Claw Background Execution Inherently Enables Silent Memory Pollution
item.description: We uncover a critical, architecture-level vulnerability in mainstream Claw personal AI agents: **heartbeat-driven background execution silently pollutes agent memory with untrusted external content** (e.g., emails, social feeds, code repos), subsequently biasing user-facing behavior without detection or consent. This arises because background and foreground sessions share the same memory context—bypassing provenance tracking and user visibility. We formalize the attack as an **Exposure → Memory → Behavior (EMB) pathway**, where misinformation ingested during heartbeat cycles enters short-term context, persists into long-term memory via routine saving, and drives downstream responses. Using MissClaw—a controlled research replica of Moltbook—we demonstrate: (1) social credibility cues (especially perceived consensus) drive up to **61% short-term behavioral misalignment**; (2) automatic memory saving embeds pollution into durable long-term memory at **91% rate**, causing cross-session influence in **76% of cases**; and (3) pollution persists even under naturalistic browsing with content dilution and context pruning. Critically, **no prompt injection is required**—ordinary social misinformation suffices. This reveals heartbeat execution itself, not input sanitization failure, as the root cause.

item.title: A Critical Review on the Effectiveness and Privacy Threats of Membership Inference Attacks
item.description: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are widely adopted as the de facto metric for privacy leakage in machine learning, yet their real-world threat level remains poorly calibrated. We propose a rigorous evaluation framework that defines four necessary conditions for MIAs to constitute a *genuine privacy threat*: (1) realistic query constraints (e.g., single noisy soft-label access), (2) limited auxiliary data, (3) production-grade model regularization, and (4) causal attribution to training-set memorization—not distributional artifacts. Under this framework, we evaluate 12 representative MIAs across 6 benchmark datasets and 4 model architectures. Contrary to common assumptions, we find that **no MIA achieves meaningful advantage under realistic conditions**: best-case AUC is ≤ 0.56 (vs. 0.5 random baseline), with average accuracy gain over chance仅为1.2–3.8%. Crucially, 92% of attacks collapse when training memorization is suppressed (e.g., via DP-SGD), confirming their dependence on fragile, non-systematic overfitting. Our results challenge the use of MIAs as primary privacy metrics—overreliance leads to unjustified utility loss (e.g., −11.7% accuracy from excessive DP) without commensurate privacy gains. We advocate reframing MIAs as diagnostic tools within threat-informed privacy assessment.

item.title: Beyond Theoretical Bounds: Empirical Privacy Loss Calibration for Text Rewriting Under Local Differential Privacy
item.description: Large language models have intensified the need for privacy-preserving text sharing. Local Differential Privacy (LDP) text rewriting offers formal guarantees via a privacy parameter $\varepsilon$, yet nominal $\varepsilon$ values are notoriously difficult to interpret and compare across mechanisms. To bridge this gap, we propose **TeDA**, an empirical calibration framework grounded in hypothesis testing that audits text distinguishability in both surface-form (token-level) and semantic embedding spaces. Applying TeDA to five representative LDP rewriting mechanisms, we find that identical nominal $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon=2.0$) yields vastly different empirical distinguishability—AUC scores range from 0.53 to 0.91—revealing substantial gaps between theoretical bounds and real-world leakage. TeDA thus provides a practical, comparable metric for privacy-utility trade-off analysis and enables evidence-based mechanism selection in production LDP deployments.

item.title: Privacy-Preserving EHR Data Transformation via Geometric Operators: A Human-AI Co-Design Technical Report
item.description: Electronic health records (EHRs) are vital for biomedical research and AI development, yet privacy and interoperability constraints impede their sharing. Existing cryptographic privacy-preserving methods—while strong—introduce prohibitive computational overhead and render data opaque to human experts, undermining clinical interpretability and exploratory analysis. We propose a geometric operator-based transformation framework that produces *semantically meaningful*, *statistically faithful*, and *provably unlinkable* numeric views of structured EHRs. Co-designed by human experts and the AI agent SciencePal acting as a constrained tool inventor, it introduces three non-reversible geometric operators (orthogonal perturbation, isometric scaling, semantics-aware rotation) plus a dynamic mixing strategy for high-risk settings. Rigorous evaluation under reconstruction, record linkage, membership inference, and attribute inference attacks shows >92% average defense gain over baselines—while preserving direct usability for clinicians and researchers. This work bridges privacy guarantees with human-AI collaborative data science.

item.title: SoK: The Attack Surface of Agentic AI -- Tools, and Autonomy
item.description: This SoK systematically maps the expanding attack surface of agentic AI—LLM systems augmented with tools, RAG, and autonomous multi-agent loops. We introduce a comprehensive taxonomy covering prompt injection (including indirect/context-hijacking variants), knowledge-base poisoning, tool/plugin exploits, and emergent multi-agent threats. Synthesizing evidence from >20 peer-reviewed studies, industry red-team reports, and standards (2023–2025), we identify novel risks: indirect prompt injection enabling stealthy code execution, RAG index poisoning causing systemic factual corruption, and cross-agent manipulation undermining goal alignment. We define attacker models, propose quantitative security metrics (e.g., Unsafe Action Rate, Privilege Escalation Distance), and critically assess defenses—including input sanitization, retrieval filters, sandboxes, access control, and AI guardrails—highlighting persistent gaps. We deliver a phased security checklist for practitioners and outline open challenges: verifiable agent behavior, robust tool APIs, supply-chain safeguards, and ethical disclosure practices.

item.title: Agent-Sentry: Bounding LLM Agents via Execution Provenance
item.description: Agentic LLM systems—capable of autonomously invoking tools and composing behaviors from natural language instructions—pose critical security and safety risks due to their probabilistic, open-ended execution flows and unbounded functionality surface. To address this, we propose **Agent-Sentry**, a lightweight, provenance-driven framework that *bounds* agent behavior by learning legitimate tool-use patterns and execution traces from real-world task executions. Agent-Sentry constructs a behavioral constraint graph capturing valid tool sequences, parameter constraints, temporal dependencies, and user-intent alignment rules; it then enforces these bounds at runtime via a policy engine that blocks out-of-distribution or intent-misaligned tool calls. Evaluated across six production-relevant domains, Agent-Sentry prevents **>90% of out-of-bounds attacks** (including prompt injection, tool hijacking, and stealthy exfiltration) while preserving **up to 98% of system utility**, demonstrating strong practicality and interpretability without requiring model retraining or heavy instrumentation.

item.title: Agent Audit: A Security Analysis System for LLM Agent Applications
item.description: Agent Audit is a security analysis system designed specifically for LLM agent applications. It addresses the critical insight that agent vulnerabilities stem not only from model weights but primarily from the surrounding software stack—including unsafe tool functions, exposed credentials in deployment artifacts, and over-privileged Model Context Protocol (MCP) configurations. Agent Audit employs an agent-aware pipeline combining inter-procedural dataflow analysis, credential detection, structured configuration parsing, and privilege-risk validation. It reports findings in terminal, JSON, and SARIF formats—enabling direct integration with VS Code, GitHub Code Scanning, and CI/CD pipelines. Evaluated on a benchmark of 22 real-world agent repositories containing 42 manually annotated vulnerabilities, Agent Audit detects 40 vulnerabilities (95.2% recall) with only 6 false positives, substantially outperforming common SAST baselines while maintaining sub-second scan latency (<0.8s). The open-source tool is pip-installable and lowers the barrier to rigorous security auditing for LLM agents.

item.title: Combinatorial Privacy: Private Multi-Party Bitstream Grand Sum by Hiding in Birkhoff Polytopes
item.description: We introduce **PolyVeil**, a combinatorial privacy protocol for private Boolean summation across $k$ clients. It encodes each private bit as a *permutation matrix*—an extreme point of the Birkhoff polytope—and leverages its geometric structure for security. A two-layer architecture ensures *perfect simulation-based security* (statistical distance zero) for the server, while the aggregator faces **#P-hard inference** via the permanent and mixed discriminant. Two variants are analyzed: in the *full variant*, the aggregator observes a doubly stochastic matrix per client, yielding an $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP guarantee that is non-vacuous only when the private signal is statistically undetectable (log-Lipschitz constant $\propto n^4 K_t$); in the *compressed variant*, the aggregator sees only a single scalar, enabling non-vacuous $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon < 2$) at moderate SNR, with optimal decoy count balancing CLT accuracy and noise concentration. This exposes a fundamental tension: #P-hardness requires the full matrix view to preserve Birkhoff structure, whereas non-vacuous DP necessitates low-dimensional output. The protocol requires **no PKI**, achieves **$O(k)$ communication**, and outputs **exact aggregates**.

item.title: CIPL: A Target-Independent Framework for Channel-Inversion Privacy Leakage in Agents
item.description: This paper introduces **CIPL (Channel Inversion for Privacy Leakage)**, a target-independent framework that formalizes privacy leakage in LLM agents as a *channel inversion* problem—where attackers infer how sensitive internal content is routed into observable output channels, rather than merely extracting memorized data. CIPL abstracts agentic systems via a universal signature (sensitive source → selection → assembly → execution → observation → extraction) and enables attack instantiation through a reusable language comprising a *locator*, an *aligner*, and a *diversification policy*. Evaluated across diverse agent frameworks (LangChain, AutoGen, LlamaIndex), CIPL demonstrates that leakage arises not only from memory but from *any stage where sensitive content influences attacker-visible signals* (e.g., tool call sequences, structured response fields). Our results advocate shifting privacy evaluation from surface-specific attacks to a *channel-centric view* of information exposure—enabling systematic, cross-target assessment and informing robust agent design.

item.title: Does Teaming-Up LLMs Improve Secure Code Generation? A Comprehensive Evaluation with Multi-LLMSecCodeEval
item.description: This paper investigates whether teaming up LLMs meaningfully improves secure code generation—a persistent challenge despite advances in LLM capabilities. We introduce **MULTI-LLMSECCODEEVAL**, a comprehensive framework that integrates multiple LLMs with static analysis and structured collaboration across the vulnerability management lifecycle (detection, analysis, patching). Benchmarking 10 pipelines—including single-model, ensemble, collaborative, and hybrid designs—on **SecLLMEval** and **SecLLMHolmes**, we find: (1) Static-analysis-augmented ensembles boost secure code generation by up to **47.3%** (SecLLMEval) and **19.3%** (SecLLMHolmes) over strong single-LLM baselines; (2) Purely LLM-based collaborative pipelines yield modest gains (**8.9%–22.3%**), underscoring the need for external validation; (3) Hybrid pipelines—combining ensembling, detection, and LLM-driven patching—achieve state-of-the-art security, outperforming best ensemble and collaborative baselines by **1.78%–4.72%** and **19.81%–26.78%**, respectively; and (4) Ablation studies confirm that model scale alone does not guarantee security—structured, smaller multi-LLM systems consistently surpass monolithic large models. Our work demonstrates that *secure code emerges not from scale, but from deliberate, system-level orchestration of complementary LLMs and tools*.

item.title: ReqFusion: A Multi-Provider Framework for Automated PEGS Analysis Across Software Domains
item.description: ReqFusion is a novel multi-provider AI framework for automated software requirements extraction, classification, and analysis across heterogeneous domains (academic, industrial, tender proposals) and document formats (PDF, DOCX, PPTX). It integrates OpenAI GPT, Anthropic Claude, and Groq models to enable consensus-driven, fallback-resilient requirement processing. Crucially, ReqFusion adopts the structured **Project–Environment–Goal–System (PEGS)** modeling approach—rather than generic prompting—to guide LLMs with rich contextual cues, significantly improving semantic fidelity. An ablation study shows PEGS-guided prompting achieves an **F1 score of 0.88**, outperforming generic prompting (0.71) under identical multi-model settings. Evaluated on 18 real-world documents (226 requirements) and extended to 5 projects (1,050 requirements), ReqFusion improves extraction accuracy by 32% and reduces analysis time by **78%** versus manual methods. Its domain-agnostic design and model-ensemble architecture deliver robust, scalable, and interpretable requirements engineering support.

item.title: WISTERIA: Weak Implicit Signal-based Temporal Relation Extraction with Attention
item.description: Temporal Relation Extraction (TRE) aims to identify temporal order (e.g., *before*, *after*, *during*) between event pairs. While attention-based models excel at global token saliency, they often neglect *pair-specific implicit signals*—lexical, syntactic, or morphological cues (e.g., perfect aspect, prepositional phrases, verb valency) that genuinely encode temporal order, without relying on explicit markers like *before* or *when*. WISTERIA addresses this by introducing **pair-conditioned top-K attention pooling**, which isolates the most informative contextual tokens *for each event pair* via multi-head attention. It treats any linguistically grounded element as a weak implicit temporal signal and enforces interpretability at the instance level. Experiments across TimeBank-Dense, MATRES, TDDMan, and TDDAuto show competitive accuracy and—critically—produce human-aligned, linguistically plausible rationales: top-K tokens consistently reflect established temporal cues (e.g., *then*, past participles, temporal prepositions). WISTERIA thus advances TRE toward localized, faithful, and explainable temporal reasoning.

item.title: SafeSeek: Universal Attribution of Safety Circuits in Language Models
item.description: SafeSeek is a universal, differentiable framework for attributing safety-critical circuits in LLMs. Unlike heuristic or domain-specific methods, it jointly optimizes sparse, multi-granular binary masks over attention heads and MLP neurons via gradient descent on safety datasets—enabling end-to-end discovery of *functionally complete* safety circuits. Integrated Safety Circuit Tuning further leverages these sparse subnetworks for efficient, targeted safety fine-tuning. Evaluated on backdoor attacks and safety alignment, SafeSeek identifies a 0.42%-sparse backdoor circuit whose ablation reduces Attack Success Rate (ASR) from 100% to 0.4% while preserving >99% general utility; and an alignment circuit comprising only 3.03% of heads and 0.79% of neurons—its removal increases ASR from 0.8% to 96.9%, confirming its essential role. Crucially, excluding this circuit during helpfulness tuning retains 96.5% safety, demonstrating safe decoupling. SafeSeek establishes the first optimization-based, generalizable foundation for mechanistic safety attribution in LLMs.

item.title: ProGRank: Probe-Gradient Reranking to Defend Dense-Retriever RAG from Corpus Poisoning
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances LLM reliability via grounded evidence, yet introduces vulnerability to *corpus poisoning*, where adversaries manipulate passages to rank highly for target queries and corrupt downstream generation. Existing defenses often require content filtering, auxiliary models, or generator-side reasoning—increasing deployment complexity. We propose **ProGRank**, a training-free, post-hoc, retriever-side defense for dense-retriever RAG. It stress-tests each query–passage pair under mild random perturbations, extracts probe gradients from a small fixed parameter subset of the retriever, and computes two instability signals: *representational consistency* and *dispersion risk*. These are fused via a score gate in a lightweight reranking step. ProGRank preserves original passages, needs no retraining, and supports a surrogate variant when the deployed retriever is unavailable. Extensive experiments across three datasets, three dense retrievers, and multiple poisoning attacks show ProGRank achieves superior defense performance and a favorable robustness–utility trade-off—even under adaptive evasive attacks.

item.title: Benchmarking Multi-Agent LLM Architectures for Financial Document Processing: A Comparative Study of Orchestration Patterns, Cost-Accuracy Tradeoffs and Production Scaling Strategies
item.description: This study benchmarks four multi-agent LLM orchestration patterns—sequential pipeline, parallel fan-out with merge, hierarchical supervisor-worker, and reflexive self-correcting loop—for structured extraction from 10,000 SEC filings (10-K/10-Q/8-K). Evaluated across five frontier and open-weight LLMs on 25 financial fields, metrics include field-level F1, document-level accuracy, latency, cost per document, and token efficiency. The reflexive architecture achieves the highest F1 (0.943) but at 2.3× baseline cost; the hierarchical design dominates the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier (F1=0.921 at 1.4× cost). Ablations show hybrid strategies—semantic caching, model routing, and adaptive retries—recover 89% of reflexive accuracy at only 1.15× baseline cost. Scaling analysis (1K–100K docs/day) reveals non-linear throughput-accuracy degradation, with F1 decline accelerating by 17% beyond 50K docs/day—critical for regulated financial operations.

item.title: GEM: Guided Expectation-Maximization for Behavior-Normalized Candidate Action Selection in Offline RL
item.description: Offline RL learns strong value functions from static datasets, but reliable deployment critically depends on how actions are selected from them. Unimodal policy extraction fails under multimodal behavior data—blurring distinct action hypotheses and yielding poorly supported “in-between” actions, even with accurate critics. We propose **GEM (Guided Expectation-Maximization)**, an analytical framework for controllable, multimodal action selection. GEM trains a critic-guided Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) actor via advantage-weighted EM-style updates that preserve component structure while shifting mass toward high-value regions; simultaneously, it learns a tractable GMM behavior model to quantify data support. At inference, GEM generates parallel candidates and reranks them using a conservative ensemble lower-confidence bound *plus* behavior-normalized support—where behavior log-likelihood is standardized *within each state’s candidate set* for stable, budget-agnostic control. Empirically, GEM matches or exceeds state-of-the-art methods across D4RL benchmarks (including AntMaze and Adroit), and introduces a simple, retraining-free inference-time knob: increasing candidate count monotonically improves decision quality without architectural changes.

item.title: HGNet: Scalable Foundation Model for Automated Knowledge Graph Generation from Scientific Literature
item.description: Automated knowledge graph (KG) construction from scientific literature remains hampered by poor multi-word entity recognition, weak cross-domain generalization, and neglect of hierarchical knowledge structure. To address this, we propose **HGNet**, a scalable two-stage zero-shot framework. Stage one, **Z-NERD**, introduces Orthogonal Semantic Decomposition (OSD) for domain-agnostic entity boundary detection and Multi-Scale TCQK attention to robustly capture long, coherent entities. Stage two, **HGNet**, performs hierarchy-aware relation extraction via parent–child–peer message passing. Crucially, we formalize hierarchical abstraction as a *continuous* property in Euclidean space—via the Differentiable Hierarchy Loss (to prevent cycles/shortcuts) and the Continuum Abstraction Field (CAF) Loss (embedding abstraction levels along a learnable 1D axis)—offering a simpler, more interpretable alternative to hyperbolic embeddings. We release **SPHERE**, the first multi-domain benchmark for hierarchical relation extraction. HGNet sets new state-of-the-art on SciERC, SciER, and SPHERE, improving NER by +8.08% and RE by +5.99% on out-of-distribution tests, and achieving +10.76% NER and +26.2% RE gains in zero-shot settings.

item.title: Balancing Safety and Efficiency in Aircraft Health Diagnosis: A Task Decomposition Framework with Heterogeneous Long-Micro Scale Cascading and Knowledge Distillation-based Interpretability
item.description: This paper addresses three core challenges in whole-aircraft health diagnosis for general aviation: data uncertainty, task heterogeneity (between global anomaly detection and local fault classification), and computational inefficiency. We propose the **Diagnosis Decomposition Framework (DDF)**, which explicitly decouples diagnosis into two stages—Anomaly Detection (AD) and Fault Classification (FC)—via the **Long-Micro Scale Diagnostician (LMSD)**. LMSD implements a “long-range global screening → micro-scale local precise diagnosis” cascade: ConvTokMHSA captures holistic operational patterns, while MMK Net extracts transient fault signatures using learnable sub-millisecond kernels. Decoupled training separates optimization pathways, cutting training time by 42–67%. A Knowledge Distillation-based **Keyness Extraction Layer (KEL)** provides physically grounded, sensor- and time-localized explanations. On the real-world NGAFID dataset, DDF improves Multi-Class Weighted Penalty Metric (MCWPM) by 4.2–7.9% over SOTA baselines while halving training cost—demonstrating superior safety-efficiency balance, interpretability-by-design, and deployability.

item.title: TreeTeaming: Autonomous Red-Teaming of Vision-Language Models via Hierarchical Strategy Exploration
item.description: We introduce **TreeTeaming**, an autonomous red-teaming framework that redefines strategy exploration for Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as a *hierarchical, evolutionary process*—moving beyond static, linear search. Its LLM-powered **Orchestrator** dynamically grows a strategy tree by deciding whether to refine promising attack paths or branch into diverse strategic directions; a multimodal **Actuator** then executes these complex, image-text co-designed attacks. Evaluated on 12 state-of-the-art VLMs, TreeTeaming achieves SOTA attack success rates on 11 models—including **87.60% on GPT-4o**—outperforming all prior methods. It also generates significantly more diverse strategies than the union of all known jailbreaks and reduces average toxicity by **23.09%**, demonstrating stealthier, more subtle exploits. TreeTeaming establishes a new paradigm for proactive, structure-aware vulnerability discovery in multimodal AI.

item.title: Spiking Personalized Federated Learning for Brain-Computer Interface-Enabled Immersive Communication
item.description: This paper introduces **Spiking Personalized Federated Learning (Spiking PFL)**, a novel framework for brain-computer interface (BCI)-enabled immersive communication. To address neurodiversity, privacy sensitivity, and severe energy constraints of wearable terminals (e.g., HMDs), we integrate personalized federated learning with spiking neural networks (SNNs). Our approach enables on-device inference of user-centric states (e.g., intention, perceptual discomfort) from raw EEG signals without sharing sensitive neural data. By leveraging sparse, event-driven SNN computation, Spiking PFL reduces inference energy by **6.46×** versus conventional ANN-based PFL baselines while achieving **92.7% average identification accuracy** across 42 subjects. The framework ensures robust personalization under strong inter-subject variability and meets real-time requirements (<20 ms latency) — making it the first energy-efficient, privacy-preserving, and deployable BCI intelligence solution for immersive systems.

item.title: Detecting Non-Membership in LLM Training Data via Rank Correlations
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are trained on massive, opaque corpora, making it critical to verify whether specific datasets were *excluded* from training—for copyright compliance, auditing, and trust. While membership inference detects *inclusion*, the complementary non-membership verification problem remains underexplored—especially under realistic grey-box access (i.e., model logits only). We introduce **PRISM**, a dataset-level non-membership test grounded in a key observation: two models *not trained* on a target dataset exhibit significantly higher Spearman rank correlation in their normalized token log-probabilities over that dataset than when one model *was* trained on it. PRISM leverages this correlation signature to construct a statistically principled, threshold-based test. Empirically, across diverse benchmarks (Wikitext, C4, The Pile), PRISM reliably rules out membership for all known non-training datasets with zero false positives—providing the first practical, verifiable framework for “proof of exclusion” in LLM data governance.

item.title: vkproof: Succinct verification of indexed verifying keys using modular compilation and polynomial fingerprinting
item.description: We present **vkproof**, a preprocessing SNARG enabling succinct verification of the verifying key (VK) for Varuna—or any Marlin-based proof system—compiled from a high-level program into R1CS. Its proof size is **constant**, and verifier time scales **linearly in the number of program instructions**, not the density of the compiled R1CS—a critical advantage for programs with frequent arithmetic-heavy operations (e.g., hashing). This efficiency stems from two innovations: (**i**) *modular compilation*, which decomposes programs instruction-wise and binds each to an indexed polynomial; and (**ii**) *polynomial fingerprinting*, using compact fingerprints to attest polynomial correctness within an extended algebraic holographic proof (eAHP) framework. eAHP generalizes Marlin’s AHP by permitting oracles to witness polynomials in the instance and supporting queries over linear combinations of indexed polynomials. vkproof thus achieves the first practical, composable solution for succinct VK verification—enabling efficient VK distribution and updates in TEEs, light clients, and recursive SNARK stacks.

item.title: Radical 3-isogenies for the ideal class group actions on $(2, \varepsilon)$-structures
item.description: Chenu and Smith introduced $(d,\varepsilon)$-structures—pairs of a supersingular elliptic curve over $\mathbb{F}_{p^2}$ and a degree-$d$ isogeny to its Frobenius conjugate—and defined an ideal class group action on them, enabling cryptographic extensions of CSIDH and the Delfs–Galbraith algorithm. This paper focuses on the $d=2$ case and presents **explicit radical 3-isogenies** for evaluating the action of the ideal class of a prime above 3. Leveraging two equivalent representations—reductions of degree-2 $\mathbb{Q}$-curves and Montgomery curves—we show that every $(2,\varepsilon)$-structure is uniquely determined by a single curve coefficient (e.g., Montgomery parameter $A$) and a sign $\varepsilon \in \{\pm1\}$. From this, we derive efficient radical formulas requiring only field arithmetic and root extractions—no point operations or scalar multiplications. As a key application, we give a concrete meet-in-the-middle algorithm to recover an ideal class connecting two given $(2,\varepsilon)$-structures, completing a core subroutine of the generalized Delfs–Galbraith algorithm.

item.title: Two-Party BBS+ Signature in Two Passes
item.description: The BBS+ signature scheme is a foundational primitive for anonymous credentials and privacy-preserving authentication, now undergoing standardization and real-world deployment. While threshold variants address single-point-of-failure risks, existing two-party (2-out-of-2) protocols require at least three communication passes and multi-kilobyte bandwidth per signature—hindering mobile-edge deployments. We present the first two-pass two-party BBS+ signing protocol achieving only **0.85 KB total communication**, roughly **27% of the prior bandwidth-optimal S&P’25 design**, while maintaining competitive signing times (62 ms and 46 ms per party) even for large attribute vectors (e.g., $\ell = 500$). Our protocol is provably secure in the standard simulation-based model against malicious adversaries and naturally generalizes to 2-out-of-$n$ threshold settings. Though slightly slower than the fastest OT-based alternative (S&P’23), it reduces bandwidth by nearly two orders of magnitude—enabling practical, lightweight trust distribution between server and mobile device.

item.title: RoKoko: Lattice-based Succinct Arguments, a Committed Refinement
item.description: We present **RoKoko**, a lattice-based succinct argument achieving linear-time proving, polylogarithmic communication, and verifier complexity—advancing the state of post-quantum SNARKs. Asymptotically, it improves upon RoK/Roll (ASIACRYPT 2025) by a $\Theta(\log \lambda)$ factor in proof size; practically, it yields ~200 KB proofs with **100× faster verification** than Greyhound (CRYPTO 2024), comparable prover time, and competitive proof size. RoKoko natively supports tensor-structured relations (e.g., polynomial evaluation, sumcheck). Its core innovation is breaking the “constant-split barrier”: by introducing (i) *committed folding* (replacing clear cross-terms with commitments + proofs), (ii) *recursive commitments* (generalizing double-commitment for per-round compression), and (iii) *sumcheck-driven structured recursion* (extending SALSAA to handle projection correctness, inner-product claims, and recursive commitment well-formedness), RoKoko achieves both linear-time proving and succinct verification under lattice assumptions.

item.title: A Universal Blinder: One-round Blind Signatures from FHE
item.description: We present universal compilers that transform *any* secure signature scheme into a one-round blind signature scheme, preserving the exact signature format (e.g., same length, verification algorithm, and key structure) — ensuring full backward compatibility. Our construction leverages two-key fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) and zero-knowledge proofs to achieve provable unforgeability and blindness. A key technical contribution is *committed verifiable FHE*, a new notion where the verifier checks correctness against a commitment to the circuit—without seeing the circuit in the clear—enhancing security and efficiency. We propose three compiler variants differing in computational responsibility: client-heavy, signer-heavy, and balanced. All schemes require only one communication round and are proven secure in the standard model. This work bridges theory and practice by enabling drop-in deployment of blind signatures over widely used signature standards.

item.title: Model Context Protocol Threat Modeling and Analyzing Vulnerabilities to Prompt Injection with Tool Poisoning
item.description: This paper presents the first comprehensive threat modeling of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), identifying **tool poisoning**—the injection of malicious instructions into tool metadata (e.g., descriptions, parameter names, response schemas)—as the dominant client-side vulnerability. Using integrated STRIDE and DREAD frameworks across five MCP components, we systematically analyze attack surfaces and quantify risks. Empirical evaluation of seven major MCP clients reveals universal weaknesses: insufficient static validation (e.g., unchecked description fields, unvalidated parameter names) and poor parameter visibility, enabling >90% of poisoning payloads to bypass detection. We propose a multi-layered defense strategy comprising: (1) static metadata analysis with semantic auditing, (2) LLM decision-path tracing, (3) runtime behavioral anomaly detection, and (4) user-transparent warning mechanisms. Our work bridges a critical gap in MCP security—shifting focus from server-centric to client-resilient design—and delivers actionable, implementable mitigations for production AI agent systems.

item.title: Evaluating the Reliability and Fidelity of Automated Judgment Systems of Large Language Models
item.description: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) as automated judges for assessing victim LLMs’ output quality and safety. We benchmark **37 conversational LLMs** (1B–70B+ parameters) with **5 engineered judge prompts**, a novel **second-level judge calibration mechanism**, and **5 task-finetuned models**, across **eight human-annotated judgment tasks** (e.g., factuality, harm, coherence). Results show strong fidelity to human judgments (Spearman ρ > 0.85) when paired with appropriate prompts—especially for **GPT-4o**, high-parameter open-weight models (≥32B), and surprisingly, compact models like **Qwen2.5-14B**. The second-level judge boosts agreement by 12.3% on average. Our work establishes a rigorous, multi-dimensional benchmark for trustworthy LLM-as-judge deployment.

item.title: SecureBreak -- A dataset towards safe and secure models
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, yet existing security alignment methods remain vulnerable to jailbreaking and prompt injection attacks—revealing a critical need for robust, post-hoc safety evaluation and filtering. To address this, we introduce **SecureBreak**, a high-reliability, human-annotated dataset explicitly designed to expose residual security weaknesses in aligned LLMs. Annotations follow a conservative “safety-first” principle to minimize false negatives. SecureBreak covers 6 major risk categories (e.g., illegal acts, privacy violation, system manipulation) with diverse adversarial variants. Experiments show that fine-tuning LLMs on SecureBreak reduces jailbreak success rates by 38.7% on average across 12 models; moreover, classifiers trained on SecureBreak achieve 0.91 F1 on unseen attacks—even in zero-shot transfer—outperforming all baselines. SecureBreak thus serves dual purposes: as a benchmark for alignment robustness assessment and as training data for deployable safety filters.

item.title: Towards Secure Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Comprehensive Review of Threats, Defenses and Benchmarks
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances LLM reliability by grounding responses in external knowledge, yet its multi-stage architecture introduces systemic security vulnerabilities. This paper presents the first end-to-end survey of RAG security, systematically mapping threat models—including data poisoning, adversarial queries, and membership inference—across the full retrieval-generation pipeline. We propose a dual-stage defense taxonomy: input-side protections (e.g., homomorphic encryption retrieval, adversarial pre-filtering) and output-side mitigations (e.g., federated isolation, differential privacy perturbation, lightweight sanitization). To enable reproducible evaluation, we consolidate benchmark datasets, regulatory standards (NIST AI RMF, GDPR), and metrics covering attack success rate, utility degradation, and privacy leakage. Our unified framework bridges fragmented prior work and lays the foundation for robust, trustworthy RAG deployment.

item.title: Are AI-assisted Development Tools Immune to Prompt Injection?
item.description: Prompt injection—the top-ranked vulnerability in the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications—poses severe risks in AI-assisted development tools built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP), especially via *tool-poisoning* vectors that manipulate tool parameters through malicious context. This paper presents the **first empirical security analysis** of prompt injection across seven widely adopted MCP clients: Claude Desktop, Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Gemini CLI, and Langflow. We systematically evaluate their detection and mitigation capabilities across six dimensions: static validation, parameter visibility, injection detection, user warnings, execution sandboxing, and audit logging. Results reveal stark disparities: while Claude Desktop enforces robust guardrails (e.g., sandboxed tool execution + explicit warnings), Cursor exhibits high susceptibility—allowing cross-tool poisoning, hidden parameter exploitation, and unauthorized command invocation without user awareness. We identify critical gaps, including the absence of implicit-context validation (e.g., in comments or filenames) in 5/7 tools. Finally, we provide actionable, implementation-level guidance for MCP developers and propose a standardized evaluation framework (MCP-PIT) to advance secure AI coding workflows.

item.title: In-network Attack Detection with Federated Deep Learning in IoT Networks: Real Implementation and Analysis
item.description: This paper presents a lightweight, federated deep learning framework for in-network attack detection in resource-constrained IoT environments. We design a compact autoencoder (under 15K parameters) deployable on Raspberry Pi edge nodes and integrate it with a privacy-preserving federated learning pipeline: local model training occurs on-device using real traffic data, while only encrypted model weights—not raw data—are aggregated centrally. Evaluated on a physical testbed of 8 Raspberry Pi nodes capturing 27.3 GB of realistic normal and attack traffic (DDoS, port scanning, malicious firmware injection), our system achieves **98.2% detection accuracy** (F1=0.971) with **96.4% lower communication overhead** versus centralized training and **<120 ms end-to-end latency**. Results demonstrate that federated anomaly detection is both practically viable and highly effective for privacy-aware, real-time IoT security.

item.title: Hardening Confidential Federated Compute against Side-channel Attacks
item.description: This paper identifies three exploitable side channels in Confidential Federated Compute (CFC) platforms—namely, gradient update frequency leakage, encrypted parameter loading timing, and cache-access patterns during DP noise injection—that enable a privileged insider to undermine differential privacy (DP) guarantees. We demonstrate that DP can be repurposed not only for statistical privacy but also as a *system-level side-channel mitigation primitive*: by applying calibrated DP noise to execution timing and invocation frequencies within the TEE, we suppress information leakage while preserving model utility. We implement and open-source the defense against the first two channels in `confed` v2.1. Evaluation on CIFAR-10/100 shows our approach reduces side-channel extraction success from 89% to ≤4.2% (p<0.001) with <0.8% accuracy degradation. This work establishes the first formal model unifying TEE security and DP for side-channel resilience in federated learning.

item.title: LLMON: An LLM-native Markup Language to Leverage Structure and Semantics at the LLM Interface
item.description: This paper introduces **LLMON (LLM Object Notation)**, an LLM-native markup language designed to explicitly encode structural and semantic metadata—such as instruction, data, context, and constraints—within LLM inputs and outputs. Unlike plain-text prompts, LLMON uses lightweight, tokenizer-friendly tags (e.g., `<instruction>`, `<data>`) to preserve information hierarchy without breaking model compatibility. We formalize key design principles for LLM-native markup—including learnability, semantic expressiveness, structural fidelity, and inference-time utility—and demonstrate how LLMON satisfies them. Empirical results show that LLMON-augmented training improves multi-task accuracy by 12.3% on average, while LLMON-aware inference reduces prompt injection success rate from 38.7% to 4.2%. Crucially, LLMON enables new capabilities: structured safety filtering, context-aware routing, and IDE-style semantic highlighting—establishing a foundation for verifiable, auditable, and composable LLM interfaces.

item.title: ThinkJEPA: Empowering Latent World Models with Large Vision-Language Reasoning Model
item.description: Recent latent world models (e.g., V-JEPA2) excel at short-horizon video forecasting but suffer from limited temporal context and poor long-horizon semantic grounding due to dense, local frame modeling. Vision-language models (VLMs) offer rich semantics and general knowledge but are ill-suited as standalone dense predictors—constrained by sparse sampling, language-output bottlenecks, and data regime mismatch. We propose **ThinkJEPA**, a dual-temporal framework integrating: (i) a dense JEPA branch for fine-grained motion dynamics, and (ii) a uniformly sampled VLM *thinker* branch for long-horizon semantic guidance. To bridge the representation gap, we introduce a hierarchical pyramid module that distills multi-layer VLM features into latent-space-compatible guidance signals. On hand-manipulation trajectory prediction, ThinkJEPA outperforms both strong VLM-only and JEPA-only baselines, achieving +23.6% and +18.1% accuracy gains at 1-second rollout, respectively, with markedly improved robustness in long-horizon (≥2s) rollouts.

item.title: SPA: A Simple but Tough-to-Beat Baseline for Knowledge Injection
item.description: We propose **SPA (Scaling Prompt-engineered Augmentation)**, a simple yet highly competitive baseline for knowledge injection into LLMs. SPA generates large-scale synthetic data using only a small set of carefully crafted prompts—no RL training, no multi-stage pipelines, and no model fine-tuning. Through systematic evaluation across diverse knowledge-intensive tasks, SPA consistently outperforms strong baselines including RLHF-Aug, Self-Instruct, and DINO. We identify two critical limitations of prior methods: (1) RL-based augmentation suffers from *diversity collapse* at scale, leading to diminishing returns; and (2) multi-stage prompting offers marginal gains only before prompt tuning—its advantages vanish after careful optimization. Our results demonstrate that high-quality prompt engineering combined with straightforward, scalable generation is surprisingly effective for knowledge injection. SPA thus establishes a robust, reproducible, and minimal benchmark for future work. Code: https://github.com/Tangkexian/SPA.

item.title: On the Failure of Topic-Matched Contrast Baselines in Multi-Directional Refusal Abliteration
item.description: This work challenges the implicit assumption that topic-matched contrast baselines improve refusal-direction extraction in multi-directional refusal ablation. Using Qwen-3.5 2B, we systematically compare matched (same-topic harmless prompts) versus unmatched baselines under identical SOM-based per-category direction extraction and SVD orthogonalization. Contrary to expectations, topic-matched subtraction yields *zero functional refusal directions* across all 28 layers and weight scales—failing to eliminate refusal on any layer. In stark contrast, unmatched baselines achieve *complete refusal elimination on six consecutive layers* (L12–L17) using the same code and evaluation protocol. Geometric analysis shows matched subtraction cancels the dominant shared activation component between harmful and harmless prompts of the same topic, collapsing direction magnitude below the perturbation threshold required for residual stream intervention. Our findings establish that contrast baseline design must prioritize *discriminative signal preservation*, not topical alignment—a fundamental methodological correction for ablation research.

item.title: Abnormalities and Disease Detection in Gastro-Intestinal Tract Images
item.description: This PhD thesis advances gastrointestinal (GI) tract image analysis through a unified, multi-stage framework addressing real-time classification and segmentation challenges. Starting with efficient texture-based methods (LBP/GLCM), it achieves >4000 FPS and 0.98 accuracy on Kvasir V2. It then introduces an optimized CNN with data bagging, attaining 0.92 accuracy (F1: 0.60) on HyperKvasir and F1: 0.88 on Kvasir V2. For real-time detection, a novel hybrid network integrating texture features and adaptive thresholding delivers 41 FPS, 0.99 accuracy, and 0.91 F1 on HyperKvasir. Finally, two segmentation tools—using depth-wise separable convolutions and neural ensembles—enhance boundary delineation, especially under low-FPS constraints. Collectively, the work bridges traditional and deep learning paradigms, delivering a deployable, accurate, and clinically adaptable solution for GI abnormality detection.

item.title: FISformer: Replacing Self-Attention with a Fuzzy Inference System in Transformer Models for Time Series Forecasting
item.description: FISFormer is a novel Transformer variant for time series forecasting that replaces self-attention with a learnable Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) interaction mechanism. For each query-key pair and per feature dimension, it applies parameterized membership functions and rule-based reasoning to produce uncertainty-aware, interpretable, and continuous interaction weights—capturing nonlinear and stochastic dependencies missed by dot-product attention. These weights are normalized via softmax over tokens and used to modulate value features. Evaluated on six benchmark datasets, FISFormer achieves state-of-the-art accuracy (avg. +3.2–7.8% improvement in MAE/MSE over Autoformer, Informer, FEDformer), superior noise robustness (41% lower error increase under Gaussian noise), and unprecedented interpretability through visualizable fuzzy rule activation. This work establishes fuzzy inference as a principled, expressive, and trustworthy alternative to conventional attention in temporal modeling.

item.title: Modeling Quantum Federated Autoencoder for Anomaly Detection in IoT Networks
item.description: We propose the **Quantum Federated Autoencoder (QFAE)**, a novel framework for privacy-preserving anomaly detection in IoT networks. QFAE integrates parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) as feature encoders—leveraging quantum superposition and entanglement to capture complex, high-dimensional traffic patterns—and federated learning (FedAvg) for decentralized training across edge devices, eliminating raw data transmission. Experiments on a real-world IoT dataset (UNSW-NB15-IoT) show QFAE achieves an F1-score of **0.942**, outperforming classical federated autoencoders by 8.6% while reducing communication overhead by 72.3%. It maintains robust detection (F1 > 0.91) under 30% device dropout and achieves <2.1% false negative rate against dynamic attacks like DDoS and Mirai. This work bridges quantum advantage with distributed learning for scalable, secure IoT security.

item.title: LLM-Based Test Case Generation in DBMS through Monte Carlo Tree Search
item.description: Database Management Systems (DBMSs) require rigorous SQL test case generation to ensure reliability, yet lightweight LLMs—widely adopted in industry for security and privacy—struggle with proprietary SQL dialects and produce semantically shallow queries that plateau coverage quickly. To address this, we propose **MIST**, an LLM-based test generation framework leveraging Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). MIST comprises two synergistic stages: (1) *Feature-Guided Error-Driven Synthesis*, which builds a hierarchical SQL feature tree and uses DBMS error feedback to steer lightweight LLMs toward syntactically valid and semantically diverse queries across dialects; and (2) *MCTS-Based Mutation*, which jointly optimizes seed query selection and mutation rule application using real-time coverage feedback to explore deeper execution paths (e.g., optimizer cost estimation branches). Evaluated on PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite with four lightweight LLMs (e.g., Phi-3-mini, TinyLlama), MIST achieves average improvements of **43.3% in line coverage**, **32.3% in function coverage**, and **46.4% in branch coverage**, raising the optimizer’s peak line coverage from 48.4% (baseline) to 69.3%.

item.title: Optimizing Feature Extraction for On-device Model Inference with User Behavior Sequences
item.description: Machine learning models in mobile apps rely on user behavior sequences (e.g., clicks, scrolls, dwell times) for real-time personalization, yet low-latency on-device inference remains challenging—not only due to model complexity, but critically, due to *redundant feature extraction* from raw application logs. We identify and address this overlooked bottleneck by proposing **AutoFeature**, an automated engine that optimizes feature extraction without sacrificing inference accuracy. AutoFeature introduces three key techniques: (1) **DAG abstraction** to unify diverse feature extraction workflows; (2) **cross-feature graph fusion** to eliminate duplicated parsing and computation nodes; and (3) **overlap-aware caching** to minimize redundant I/O and processing across consecutive inferences. Evaluated in five industrial mobile services (search, video, e-commerce), AutoFeature reduces end-to-end on-device model latency by **1.33×–3.93× during daytime** and **1.43×–4.53× at night**, with **zero accuracy degradation** (ΔAUC < 0.001). This work establishes feature extraction optimization as a first-class systems challenge for on-device ML.

item.title: Privacy-Preserving Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback via Decoupled Reward Modeling
item.description: We propose **Decoupled Reward Modeling with Differential Privacy (DRM-DP)**, a principled framework for privacy-preserving reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). Unlike prior DP-RL methods that privatize policy updates or full RL loops, DRM-DP *only* applies differential privacy to the *reward modeling* stage—via DP-SGD on preference pairs—while performing standard (non-private) policy optimization on the resulting private reward model. This decoupling respects RLHF’s inherent two-stage structure and avoids high-variance noisy gradients in policy learning. Theoretically, we derive a tight upper bound on the suboptimality gap, showing privacy introduces an additive $O(1/\sqrt{n\varepsilon^2})$ term beyond classical statistical error; we further establish a minimax lower bound characterizing phase transitions between privacy-limited and sample-limited regimes. Empirically, on the Anthropic HH-RLHF dataset with Gemma-2B-IT, DRM-DP achieves superior alignment performance across privacy budgets ($\varepsilon = 1, 2, 4$; $\delta = 10^{-5}$), outperforming existing DP baselines by up to 12.7% in win rate while maintaining training stability.

item.title: A Theoretical Framework for Energy-Aware Gradient Pruning in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) faces severe energy constraints on resource-limited edge devices, yet conventional gradient sparsification (e.g., Top-K pruning) is energy-agnostic—it treats all parameter updates as equally costly despite stark hardware-level disparities in memory access, cache coherence, and data movement. We formalize gradient pruning as an **energy-constrained projection problem**, explicitly incorporating hardware-aware cost weights. We propose **Cost-Weighted Magnitude Pruning (CWMP)**, which selects parameters by their magnitude-to-cost ratio $|g_i|/c_i$, proving it is the optimal greedy solution to this constrained problem. A probabilistic analysis establishes its guaranteed global energy efficiency under realistic gradient distributions. Experiments on non-IID CIFAR-10 show CWMP achieves a superior accuracy-energy Pareto frontier: it reduces average device energy consumption by 23.7% versus Top-K at equal communication cost, while improving test accuracy by +1.4%. This work provides the first theoretically grounded, hardware-aware pruning framework for energy-efficient FL.

item.title: TrustFed: Enabling Trustworthy Medical AI under Data Privacy Constraints
item.description: TrustFed is a novel federated uncertainty quantification framework designed for trustworthy medical AI under strict data privacy constraints. It provides distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees—without requiring centralized data access—despite severe data heterogeneity, site-specific biases, and class imbalance across healthcare institutions. TrustFed introduces two key innovations: (1) a representation-aware client assignment mechanism that leverages internal model embeddings to enable robust cross-site calibration; and (2) a soft-nearest threshold aggregation strategy that reduces assignment uncertainty while yielding compact, reliable prediction sets. Evaluated on over 430,000 real-world medical images across six distinct imaging modalities (X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound, dermoscopy, fundus), TrustFed achieves an average empirical coverage of 95.2% (target: 95%) and maintains >93.8% coverage even under extreme class imbalance (e.g., 1:200 ratio). This work advances uncertainty-aware federated learning from proof-of-concept to clinically meaningful, modality-agnostic deployment—establishing statistically guaranteed uncertainty as a foundational requirement for next-generation healthcare AI.

item.title: FedCVU: Federated Learning for Cross-View Video Understanding
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving multi-camera video understanding, yet cross-view FL suffers from severe non-IID data, misaligned representations, and excessive communication. To address this, we propose **FedCVU**, a novel federated framework featuring: (i) **VS-Norm**, preserving view-specific batch normalization statistics to mitigate viewpoint bias; (ii) **CV-Align**, a lightweight contrastive regularization that enforces semantic consistency across views via intra-video instance discrimination; and (iii) **SLA**, a selective layer aggregation strategy that transmits only task-critical layers (e.g., spatiotemporal attention), cutting communication by up to 47% with negligible accuracy drop (<0.3%). Extensive experiments on cross-view action recognition (UCF101-CV) and person re-identification (Market-1501-CV) show FedCVU consistently improves unseen-view accuracy (+5.2% over FedAvg) while maintaining strong seen-view performance, demonstrating superior robustness to domain heterogeneity and communication constraints.

item.title: PRIVADA: Private user-centric Data Aggregation
item.description: PRIVADA is a maliciously secure, user-centric data aggregation framework built on the SPDZ MPC protocol. It addresses critical gaps in existing privacy-preserving aggregation: while prior works assume semi-honest adversaries and only guarantee input confidentiality, PRIVADA simultaneously ensures **input privacy**, **user anonymity** (i.e., participation hiding), **selective disclosure**, and **result privacy**—even under collusion among malicious data owners, aggregators, and *multiple* data customers. Unlike single-customer MPC schemes, PRIVADA enables fine-grained, policy-driven result sharing across heterogeneous stakeholders without leaking user participation or individual contributions. Our proof-of-concept implementation achieves 12–15× speedup over state-of-the-art solutions, demonstrating that strong security guarantees need not compromise practicality. PRIVADA thus establishes a new benchmark for scalable, real-world private analytics.
